3.happening: Building the dp table with {n} + 1 slots, all starting at zero.
3.why: Each slot i will hold the number of distinct ways to reach step i.
3.invariant: dp[i] always means the number of ways to reach step i.

4.why: There is exactly one way to be at the bottom — take no steps at all.
4.invariant: The base cases anchor every later value.

5.why: There is exactly one way to reach step 1 — a single 1-step.
5.invariant: Steps 0 and 1 are now known for certain.

6.happening: Advancing the pointer to fill step {i} of the table.
6.why: Each step's count is built from the two steps just below it.
6.invariant: Every step below i is already final.

6.happening: No steps left to fill — the table is already complete.
6.why: n is small enough that the base cases already hold the answer.

7.happening: Combining the two ways below: dp[{i - 1}] ({dp[i - 1]}) + dp[{i - 2}] ({dp[i - 2]}).
7.why: Every path to step i ends with either a 1-step from step i-1 or a 2-step from step i-2.
7.invariant: dp[i] counts every distinct path to step i.

8.happening: Reading the final count from the last slot, dp[{n}].
8.why: The last slot holds the number of ways to reach the very top.

9.happening: Returning {result} distinct ways to climb {n} stairs.
9.why: The table is fully built, so the count is final.
9.invariant: The answer counts every distinct climb to the top.

init.happening: Setting up the table and its two base cases.
init.why: The recurrence needs the two smallest cases to start from.
init.invariant: dp[i] means the number of ways to reach step i.

loop.happening: Walking up the staircase one step at a time.
loop.why: Each new step depends only on the two steps below it.
loop.invariant: Steps below the pointer are already final.

update.happening: Filling a step's count from the two steps below it.
update.why: Paths to step i arrive from step i-1 or step i-2.
update.invariant: dp[i] counts all distinct paths to step i.

return.happening: Returning the total number of distinct climbs.
